sutta: buddha said monks these three things must be born in mind so long as he lives by a raja a duly anointed ruler what three the first is the place where the raja the duly anointed ruler was born then again monks the second thing he must bear in mind so long as he lives is the place where he was anointed that means where he was crown king then again monks the third thing he must bear in mind so long as he lives is the place where he won a battle the place which he occupies as conqueror in the fight these are the three things in like manner monks these three things must be born in mind by a monk so long as he lives what three the place where he got his hair and beard shaved off dawn the saffron ropes and went forth a wanderer from home to the homeless life this is the first thing he must bear in mind so long as he lives then again monks the second thing he must bear in mind is the place where he realized as it really is the meaning of this is ill or this dukkha this is the rising of dukkha this is the seizing of duka this is the practice that leads to the ending of dukkha then again monks the third thing he must bear in mind so long as he lives is the place where by the destruction of the asavas he himself in this very life came to know thoroughly deliberation by mind and liberation by wisdom that is without asavas and having attained it abides therein these three things monks must be born in mind by a monk so long as he lives this uttar.
commentary: basically only concerns monks and i've read it basically for monks and that these three things that monk must always remember where he went forth as a monk the second one where he understood the four uh uh aryan truths the basics of the aryan truths that means he entered the stream and the third one is where he became an arahant

items: the place where he got his hair and beard shaved off
the place where he realized as it really is
the place where by the destruction of the asavas he himself in this very life came to know thoroughly deliberation by mind and liberation by wisdom

sutta: Monks possessed of three qualities among his proficient in the practice leading to the sure cause and he has strong grounds for the destruction of the asavas what three herein among practices since restrained in riya sangwara he is moderate in eating bhojani matanyuta and is devoted to wakefulness jagaryanu yoga and how does he practice sense restraint herein among seeing an object with the eye does not grasp at the general features or at the details thereof since coveting and dejection evil and profitable states might overwhelm one who dwells with the faculty of the eye uncontrolled he applies himself to such control restrains the faculty of eye attains control thereof when he hears a sound with the ear or with his nose smells ascend or with the tongue taste of the flavor or with the body contacts tangibles but with the mind he cognizes mental states he does not grasp at the met at the general features or details thereof but since coveting and dejection evil unprofitable states might overwhelm him he restrains the faculty of mind attains control thereof that monks is how among practices sends refrain and how is among moderate and eating herein a monk takes his food thoughtfully and prudently not for fun not for indulgence not for fattening not for beautification only for the maintenance and nourishment of the body for keeping it unharmed to help the practice of the holy life thinking my former feeling i checked and i set going no new feeling so shall i keep going be blameless and live happily thus is among moderate in eating and how is a monk devoted to wakefulness herein by day a monk walks up and down and then sits thus cleansing his heart of things that he must check by night for the for the first watch he does likewise in the middle watch of the night lying on his right side he takes up the line posture resting one foot on the other and thus collected and composed fixes his thoughts on rising up again in the last watch of the night at early dawn he walks up and down then sits and soul cleanses his heart of things that he must check that is how among is given to wakefulness possess of these three qualities among is provision in the practice leading to the sure cause and he is thoroughly set upon the destruction of the asavas.
commentary:
the buddha is talking about three practices which are very very important for among the first one is practicing sense restraint sense restraint that means he does not look at the features or the details of of the sixth sense objects only what is uh necessary here he will notice in other words um he tries not to to pay attention to the things of the world the buddha taught us in meditation to practice the four stations of mindfulness four objects of sati patana which is basically uh body and mind the mind is a partner which is very very important and we should always focus our attention on the mind because if you watch the mind carefully you will be able to know that the body is also in the mind and then the second one is moderation in eating not to eat too much the third one is practicing wakefulness during the day and night and try to to practice sitting and walking except for the middle watch the middle watch is supposed to be from about 10 pm to 2 am that means if you can sleep 4 hours but then 4 hours is not something that everybody can do only somebody who's got extremely strong samadhi might be able to do with four hours but if we can do with not more than six hours that should be quite all right because even in yoga this the advice that we should sleep not more than six hours last week we came to suta number 
      
      three point two point sixteen and in this suta the buddha was talking about three things to be practiced and the buddha said that if you if a monk is proficient in the practice of these three things he has strong grounds for the destruction of the assad i went through these three things the other day but i'd like to commend a bit more about them the first one i said was a practices sense restraint indriya sankvara actually i made a slight mistake here the practice is actually sensory sense restraint but the title of this practice is which means guarding the sense doors in a description of guarding the sense doors the buddha says that when a monk sees an object with the eye he does not grasp at the general features that is the characteristics like nemita the minor details of it and buddha said if he did so coveting and dejection evil and profitable states might overwhelm one who dwells with the faculty of the eye uncontrolled so he sets a restrain over the faculty of eye similarly for the other six sense doors for the ear the nose tongue the body and the mind so here you we observe very carefully in this guarding of the sen's doors a person in practicing meditation is to be careful not to grasp the word here grass actually is the bollywood ghahi which means to take up or to accept or to hold on to hold on to the objects of the sixth sense doors either the general features of it the signs of it or the details of it so what the buddha is saying is that we don't take up or accept or hold on to the characteristics of the sixth sense objects all the details and we restrain the faculty of i when we restrain the faculty of i and the other six senses it means only what is necessary we pay attention to what is not necessary we don't pay attention because if we paid attention to the sixth sense objects now what would happen is that there is perception there is feeling followed by perception and followed by thoughts and these thoughts would multiply what is called papancha a proliferation of thoughts which is hard to restrain so in the buddha's teachings meditation is not to watch the sixth sense doors as some people say because here that's what it says not to watch the sick sense doors in fact to practice the restraint of the sixth sense doors what we actually have to watch is the body and mind what we take to be the cell because the problem with the living beings is that we hold on to this idea of a self and this idea of a self is tied to the body and the mind therefore to untie the knot now the knot is here like in body and mind to untie the knot we have to observe the body and not the body and the mind to see that there is no atta no self there so the body and the mind is also basically the four stations of mindfulness or the four stations of intense uh states of recollection so that's the first one the second one is as i mentioned the other day moderation in eating third one is devotion to wakefulness these are the three and the other thing i like to mention here is that in the diga nikaya there is a definition of a term called and this conduct you see the buddha one of the names of the buddha is vija charana sampano that means replete with charana conduct and vija is knowledge the buddha is replete with knowledge and conduct and so this term conduct or charana can also be called practice is uh according to the late uh lady said or he says that conduct charana consists of these few things like first one is sila seven things first one is sila moral conduct the second one is sentosa um contentment the third one is which is guarding the sense doors fourth one is satisfying recollection and awareness fifth one is viveka seclusion sixth one is near nivarana nivarana abandonment of the hindrances the seventh one is the four janas attainment of the four janas now this uh explanation of charana consisting of these seven things given by the late lady side or is actually in accordance with the jiga nikaya only the diga nikaya but if we study the other nikayas like majima nikaya and this anguttara nikaya sang yuta nikaya we had a few we have to add three more items which he did not include two of these items is in the suta we just read one is bhojani matanuta moderation and eating another one is jagaryanu yoga devotion to wakefulness uh these two is obvious from this la suta we just read that they are very important so they must not be left out in the practice of the spiritual path the third one he left out was the seven zadama the seven true dharmas seven items which are important this this seven items is mentioned in majima nikaya number 53 first one is faith or confidence in the buddha dharma and sangha the second one is hiri third one is otapa siri and otapa is conscience and remorse a sense of conscience and a sense of remorse then bahu sacha much learning much during of the dharma much knowledge of the dharmaviriya effort sati recollection or mindfulness panya is wisdom so these seven things are seven saddam are also important things to acquire on the path so a complete uh explanation of charana would be the seven seven items given in the dika nikaya and by ladies idol plus you add another three things which was missed out in the diga nikaya namely i just mentioned moderation in eating devotion to wakefulness and the seven saddam seven things which are important now you come to the next suta

sutta: the buddha said monks possessed of three characteristics a shopkeeper is incapable of acquiring wealth he had not before of holding what he gets or increasing what he holds what three here in monks the shopkeeper at early dawn attends not closely to his work nor at midday nor again at in the evening these are the three correct characteristics just so monks possess of three characteristics among is incapable of acquiring a state of profit of holding it when gotten or increasing a state of profit when he gets it what are the three hearing monks at early dawn the monk does not concentrate on the sign of his meditation exercise nor at midday nor at nor in the evening it is owing to these three characteristics that among is incapable of acquiring a state of profit of holding it etc and vice versa
commentary: so in this suta the buddha is saying that a shopkeeper because he does not attend closely to his work in the morning in the midday or in the evening so he is not capable of making much money so in the same way if a monk does not uh concentrate on his meditation in the morning in the afternoon and in the evening he's also not able to progress in his meditation this [Music] this is trying to tell us that meditation is actually a full-time job full-time duty if you want to improve in our meditation we have to uh almost the whole day try to read our mind of unwholesome states and concentrated on our meditation uh object so that if we keep on practicing the whole day then we can progress much faster so we have to remember that the more time we put into meditation the faster we would progress but if we spend very little time only once a day or half an hour a day and it's very difficult to progress

sutta: monks there are these three persons found existing in the world what three the topsy-turvy brain the scatter brain and the man of comprehensive brain of what sort monks is the topsy-turvy brain herein a certain person frequents the monastery to hear dhamma from the lips of the monks the monks teach him dhamma that is lovely in the beginning lovely in the middle lovely in the end both in spirit and in letter they make plain the holy life perfectly fulfilled in all its purity but as he sits there he pays no heed to that talk in its beginning pays no heat to its middle pays no he to its end also when he has risen from his seat he pays no heat there too just as when a pot is turned upside down the water poured thereon runs off and does not stay in the pot even so in this case a certain person frequents the monastery but pays no heed to that talk also when he rises from his his seat he pays no heat there too this one is called the topsy-turvy brain and of what sword monks is the scatter brain in this case a certain person frequents the monastery as he sits he pays heed to that talk in its beginning its middle and its end but when he has risen up from his seat he pays no heat there too just as when in a man's lap diverse kinds of food are piled together such as sesame rice sweet meats and jujube fruits when he rises from his seed he scatters all abroad through absent-mindedness even so amongst in this case a certain person frequents the monastery but when he has risen from his seat he pays no heed there too this one is called a scatter brain now what's what monks is the man of comprehensive mind in this case a certain person frequents the monastery to hear dhamma from the lips of the monks they teach him dhamma that is lovely in the beginning lovely in the middle lovely in the end both in its spirit and its letter they make plain the holy life perfectly fulfilled in all its purity as he sits there he pays heed to that talk in its beginning he pays he to that tongue in his middle he pays heed to its ending also when he rises from his seat he still bears it in mind just as when a pot is set upright the water poured therein accumulates and does not run away even so in this case a person frequents the monastery and pays heed to that talk also when he rises from his seat he bears it in in mind in its beginning in its middle and in its ending this one monks is called the man of comprehensive mind such monks are the three persons found existing in the world
commentary: i think this suta is quite plain there you can understand the topsy turvy brain person he doesn't pay attention his mind is so scattered now when he's sitting there listening to the talk he cannot concentrate on the talk then when he gets up after he leaves the monastery also he's forgotten what the talk was all about the second one he pays attention when the talk is is going on but when he goes back he does not reflect on that on the top it's all by going in one ear and gone out the other is forgotten about what was taught instead of reflecting on it the third one is he appears attention to it when the talk is delivered and even after the talk he goes back he still reflects it in his mind and he remembers what was heard so you all can examine yourselves and see which category you fall into now

sutta: monks these three persons are found existing in the world but three there is a person who is not to be followed not to be served not to be honored then there is a person who should be followed served and honored and then there is the one who is to be followed served and honored with worship and reverence now of what sword monks is the one who is not to be followed in this case a certain person is inferior to oneself in moral conduct concentration and wisdom i mean sila samadhi and panya such a one monks is not to be followed served or honored except out of consideration out of compassion for him and of what sword monks is the one who should be followed in this case a certain one is like oneself in moral conduct concentration and wisdom such a one should be followed served and honored why with this idea as we are both proficient in moral conduct our talk will be of morality and it will continue to our profit and that will be to our comfort as we are both proficient in concentration in wisdom our talk will be of these subjects and it will continue to our profit and that will be to our comfort therefore such a person should be followed served and honored and of what sort monks is the one who is to be followed served and honored with worship and reverence in this case a certain person is superior to oneself in moral conduct concentration and wisdom such a one should first be worshiped and revered then followed served and honored why so with this idea in this way i shall complete the sum total of morality not yet complete or by wisdom i shall supplement it here and there when it is complete or i shall complete the sum total of my concentration not yet complete or i shall supplement it by wisdom or insight here and there when it is complete or again i shall complete the sum total of my wisdom not yet complete or when complete i shall supplement it here and there by inside therefore such a person should be followed served and honored with worship and reverence these monks are the three persons found existing in the world
commentary: now here the buddha is talking about three persons and one is inferior to you in uh the treating sila samadhi and panya and this kind of person is to be avoided unless you have compassion for that person you want to help that person the second type of person who should be followed and whom you should keep company is somebody who is of the same standard with you in sila samadhi this type of person you can keep company like a spiritual friend a friend in the dhamma the third one is one whom you should follow and also reveal that is somebody who is superior to you and sila samadhi and panya because he can teach you to improve to complete your shila samadhi and pania so that kind of person who can be your teacher whom you can learn from you should follow him and serve him so if you want to find a teacher that's the kind of teacher you ought to look for one who is superior to you in sila samadhi and paneer the next suta is

items: one who is not to be followed
one who is to be followed served and honored with worship and reverence

sutta: monks those families where mother and father are worshiped in the home are reckoned like unto brahma those families where mother and father are worshiped in the home are ranked are ranked with the teachers of all worthy of offerings monks are those families where mother and father are worshiped in the home brahma amongst is a term for mother and father teaches of all monks is a term for mother and father worthy of offerings monks is a term for mother and father why so because mother and father do much for children they bring them up nourish and introduce them to the world
commentary: is another suta where the buddha is teaching us to have a lot of love and respect for our parents because we owe a lot to them if we have a lot of respect and love for our parents then even the divas have respect for us

sutta: thus have i heard on a certain occasion the exalted one was staying near alavi at khao path in singh sapa grove lodging on the leave strong ground now hathaka of alavi was wandering there afoot and as he went along he saw the exalted one in that place seated on the ground strong with leaves on seeing him he approached and saluting him sat down at one side so seated hathaka of allavi said this to the exalted one praise her does the exalted one live happily and the buddha replied yes my lad i live happily am one of those who live happily in the world and then hataka said but sir the winter nights are cold the dark half of the month is the time of snow fall hard is the ground trampled by the hooves of cattle thin the carpet of fallen leaves spas are the leaves of the tree cold are the saffron ropes and cold the gale of wind that blows then the exhorted one said still my lad i live happily of those who live happily in the world i am one now my lad i will question you about this and do you reply as you think fit but thank you my land suppose a house father or house father's son has a house with a gabled roof plastered inside and out with well fitting doors and casements therein is a couch spread with long fleeced woolen rug a bedspread of white wool a coverlet embroidered with flowers spread with a costly skin of antelope having a canopy overhead and a scarlet cushion at each end here is a lamb burning and four wives to wait upon him with all their charms now what thank you my lad would he live happily or not what do you think and he replied yes he would sir he is one of those who live happily in the world and the buddha said well now my land what do you think would there not arise in that house father or house father's son torments of body or mind that are that are born of passion raga so that tortured by them he would live unhappily and he replied they would arise sir buddha said well my land as to those torments of body or of mind born or passion tortured by which he would live unhappily that passion has been abandoned by the tata gather cut off at the root made like a palm tree stump made unable to become again of a nature not to arise again in future that is why i live happily again would there not arise torments or body or of mind born of hatred dosa so that tortured by them he would live unhappily and he replied they would arise sir the buddha said well my lad as to those torments that hatred has been abandoned by the tatangata that is why i live happily again would dare not arise torments of body of mind born of delusion boha so that tortured by them he would live unhappily they would arise sir well my land as to those torments etc etc that that delusion has been abandoned by the tata gata that is why i live happily
commentary: so here in this utah the buddha is living in the forest staying on on the ground sitting on the ground sleeping on the ground and in india they have winters winter nights which are so cold and yet the buddha says he's happy because he's gotten rid of the three causes of unhappiness that is passion raga hatred dosa and delusion moha these three things are sometimes in the suta is mentioned not raga dosa and moha but loba greed loba dosa and moha here sometimes it's mentioned as raga dosa moha raga is passion or lust instead of a loba is greed so that's why sometimes some people don't understand they think why does a person give up his worldly life abandoned comforts of the of the world and become a renunciant walking around bald-headed begging for his food because as it is played here even though uh a monk is not comfortable physically but mentally he's much more comfortable than a lot of lay people so his happiness is within and the the happiness that is within is of a deeper nature it's a deeper happiness than the happiness of a lot of lay people a lot of lay people the happiness depends on external things and it's so fragile you love somebody when that that person says something nasty to you and you get a heart ache just you you picture yourself to be of a certain uh you know you want people to think you are such and such and your peers are sensitized when somebody criticizes you your self-image seems to be shattered and you get so unhappy because external things if you depend on external things for your happiness yes so fragile you in depend on yourself on on your mind then it is much much more stable happiness

sutta: the buddha said monks there are these three forms of pride what three the pride of youth the pride of health the pride of life the uneducated manifold drunk with the pride of youth practices immorality indeed word and thought so doing when body breaks up after death such a one is reborn in the waist the woeful planes the downfall in hell likewise the uneducated manifold when drunk with the pride of health practices immorality indeed word and thought so doing when body breaks up after death such a one is reborn in the ways the woeful claims the downfall in hell and similarly the uneducated manifold drunk with the pride of life practices immorality indeed word and thought and so also goes down the waffle planes and then the buddha continues amongst intoxicated with the pride of youth a monk gives up the training and falls back to the low life so likewise one who is intoxicated with the pride of health gives up the training and falls back to the lowly life similarly among intoxicated with the pride of uh life uh keeps up the training and falls back to the low light and that's the end of the sutta
commentary: so in this utah the buddha says that these three things most people are proud especially when they are young and the pride of youth the pride of health when you are youthful you have very good health so you are very proud of your health also and maybe you think you're very strong your physique is very uh good looking etc and the pride of life also especially when we are young we think we never even think of death and the thought of death doesn't even occur to us so because of that lay persons are drunk with these three things youth health and life they are not willing to practice the spiritual path and sometimes if you remind them of the spiritual path they said i'll wait until they are old but they don't realize that uh all asia might be something that some of them would not even see because there's some people who pass away in the when they are young some people pass away in the middle age some people pass away in their old age and because of the being drunk with these three things uh pride of youth health and life huh they like to enjoy life and they enjoy life to the extent sometimes you see you know some people live as if life is forever and they just forget that death is not so far away if we consider that on the average we live up to about 70 and we calculate how many years we have left it's not that many years you know we have that so similarly the buddha is among also strong with these three things it gives up the training that means give the training is the the life of a monk and falls back to the low life the low life is the lay person's life left so you see sometimes uh some people become among the intention to renounce and practice but after a while they forget about it and they become very relaxed they are not interested in meditation etc and also because of uh the pride in these three things they sometimes even entertain thoughts of disrobing and they become satisfied dissatisfied with the ropes and they compare themselves with other people enjoying life and they also think of enjoying life so buddha says not to be strong with these three things and these three forms of pride we have to be very careful uh we just don't know when uh that would touch us as a as the buddha says life is uncertain but death is certain now we come to the next suta

on sorrows lamentation and woes on despair and tribulations and fallen on ill or done with ill perhaps some ending of all this mass of you may be revealed to me yet if i who went forth from thus from reflects well proclaimed by the exalted one is dharma seen in this very life a thing not involving time inviting one to come and see eating onward to be known for themselves by the wise now i have fellows in the righteous life who abide in knowledge and insight if i who am one that went forth under this well-proclaimed dhamma vinaya should dwell in sloth and negligence it would be unseemly for commentary: This suta is more for manga and these are the three ways and the buddha says that a monk can remind himself to strive one is based on the cell the other one is based on the world that somebody might know through psychic power that is lazy etc and the third one is using dhamma to remind him to strike the other thing i like to explain here is now and then we come across the word recluses and brahmins recluses and brahmins this is a translation for the pali word samana brahmana this brahmana is a cast in india in the days of the buddha there were four castles in india the priest cast was called brahmana and nowadays they are called brahmin islam and so during the buddha's time [Music] the brahmins were the priests castle then you have the warrior class the katya then you have the merchant class and you have the workers class the lower class these are the four classes so the the brahmin class they are a caste so if somebody from another cast renounces and becomes a manga he cannot call himself brahmana because that is only reserved for the brahmin caste and so any other manga who renounces uh is called a samana that is why our buddha is very often in the suta is called gotama the recluse that's a translation for samana gotama so that's what i have to say about recluses and brahmins now

sutta: the buddha said monks there are these three marks of that which is conditioned (saṅkhata). what three? its birth is apparent, its passing away is apparent, its changeability while it persists is apparent—these are the three marks of the conditioned. monks, there are these three marks of the unconditioned. what three? its birth is not apparent, its passing away is not apparent, its changeability while it persists is not apparent—these are the three marks of the unconditioned.
commentary: so in this sutta we are told that anything that is conditioned has these three characteristics: it has a birth, it has a passing away (death), and it changes while it is still around.

items: its changeability while it persists is apparent

sutta: a certain brahmin of great wealth came to see the exalted one and then paid respect etc as he said to one side he said this to the exalted one master gotama i have heard the saying of brahmins of all golden days men aged far gone in years themselves the teachers of teachers that once upon a time this world was in truth so crowded with people one might think it to be the waveless deep that villages suburbs and royal cities were close enough for a cockerel to walk from one to another pray master gautama what is the reason what is the cause of the apparent loss and decrees of human beings how is it that villagers are no longer villages suburbs no longer suburbs towns no longer towns and districts are depopulated then the buddha replied just now brahmin people are ablaze with unlawful lust overwhelmed by depraved longings obsessed by wrong doctrines thus ablaze overwhelmed and obsessed they seize sharp knives and take each other's lives thus many men come by the end that brahman is the reason why these things are so again brahman since folk are ablaze with unlawful lusts overwhelmed by depraved longings obsessed by wrong doctrines on such as these the sky rays not down steadily it is hard to get a meal the crops are a bad afflicted with mildew and grown to mere stubs accordingly many come to the end that brahman is the reason again brahmin sincefolk are ablaze with unlawful lusts etc obsessed by wrong doctrines on such miscreants the yakas lose non-human monsters thereby many come by the end this brahman is the reason this is the cause of the apparent loss and decrease of human beings that is why villages are no longer villages suburbs no longer suburbs town no longer towns and districts are depopulated and the brahmin said excellent master gautama excellent master gautama may the worthy gotama accept me as a lay follower from this day for as long as life lasts as one who has gone to him for refuge
commentary: and this is an interesting suita it tells us that long time ago the world was populated with so many people that cities that were near so near each other that a cockroach could walk from one to another city and the place was overflowing with people you know but nowadays it's much less and the reason for this according to the buddha has a few reasons the first one is because of greed hatred and delusion people go to war and kill each other then the second reason is because of green hatred and delusion and following wrong doctrines the devas they notice you remember just now the suta that about four times a month these days come and observe us and because they see that the great hatred and delusion of human beings are too great that they stop the the rains from coming down uh it seems that rains are also controlled by them so because uh the the the rains don't come at the right time uh the crops don't grow well so people starve to death and the third one is uh when greed hatred and delusion is too great among human beings and then these higher devas they let loose very fierce yakas you know and these peers yakas can come and kill human beings and you know sometimes that some people are possessed by spirits and these are some of the spirits probably [Music] so um why people can have too much green hatred and delusion partly because they follow wrong doctrines and also the leaders have to a lot to do with it it's a leaders of [Music] uh people whether they are malay persons or monks also have a part to play if leaders are corrupt then it is very easy for the followers also to be corrupt but if you have good leaders then people will also imitate them and the next utah is

sutta: the wanderer came to see the exalted one and paid respect etc then as he said to one side vacha gota the wanderer said this to the exalted one i've heard it said master gautama that gotama the recluse speaks thus arms should be given to me not to others to my followers not to another's followers arms given to me are of great fruit not so those given to others arms given to my followers are of great prophet not arms given to another's followers now master gautama do they who say these things correctly repeat the views of the worthy gotama without misrepresenting him by uttering an untruth do they expound their views in accordance with his teaching so that one who is of his doctrine of his way of thinking may not give grounds for reproach in stating it indeed we are anxious not to misrepresent the worthy goshama and the buddha replied those who say so vacha are not of my way of thinking moreover such misrepresents me by stating what is not true but a falsehood indeed bacha who so prevents another from giving arms obstructs him in three ways robs him of three things what three first he obstructs the giver in acquiring merit second he prevents the receivers from getting a gift third his own self is already ruined utterly ruined who so prevents another from giving arms bacha him in three ways robs him of three things but this is what i really do declare if one should throw away spot pot scourings or the rinsings of cups into a pool or says pit that means a pit of excrement even with the idea of feeding the creatures that live therein i declare it would be a source of merit to him to say nothing of his feeding beings that are human nevertheless i say vacha that a gift given in the case of the virtuous is of great fruit not those given in the case of the wicked by virtuous i mean one who has abandoned five qualities and possesses five qualities what are the five qualities he has abandoned sensual desire is abandoned malevolence sloth and torpor excitement and blurry doubt and wavering are abandoned these are the five and of what five qualities is he possessed he possesses the constituents of morality possessed by the adept the constituents of concentration wisdom liberation knowledge and vision of liberation he has these five qualities thus when five qualities are abandoned and five possessed a gift is of great fruit
commentary: this is uh interesting subta because here somebody is asking the buddha whether he actually said that offerings should be made to him and his followers not to some other other external sec followers like and the buddha denied it the buddha said he never said that and i said if anybody says such a thing then [Music] in three ways he has done great wrong and the buddha also says that even the crumbs are the leftover of food now even you throw into a pool or a bit of excrement with the idea of feeding the creatures there even then also you get some merit not to not to talk about feeding human beings and then lastly the buddha says that if you give to an arahant one who has abandoned the five hindrances and possessed of five other qualities of the arahant then you would have great merit [Music] now um one thing i like to remark here is that one of the wrong livelihoods for one of the wrong ways of livelihood for a manga is to belittle another monk personally and to talk bad and about another monk and discourage uh lay people from making offerings to another monk and that is very bad because here you can see that the buddha is saying even those external sex people even then also he does not say that you should not make offerings to them even external sex people even with wrong views and the buddha in fact there's one suta where the followers of a jain said i think mikantas after they left the nigantas and came to follow the buddha the buddha still advised them to continue giving to those niganta and so so you can see here that anyone practicing the spiritual path and we should not be little others even though they are i mean not to the extent of asking people not to make offerings to these other people and
